---
title: "01"
format: html
editor: visual
---

\# **Introduction to Human Biology Laboratory Workbook Version 4**

\## **George Mateja**

The Community College of Baltimore County

![](_page_0_Picture_3.jpeg)

Introduction to Human Biology Laboratory Workbook; an Open Education Resource. First Edition © 2024 by George Mateja is licensed under CC BY-SA 4.0. To view a copy of this license, visit <https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/>

\# **Table of Contents**

| Acknowledgements iii                                               |     |
|--------------------------------------------------------------------|-----|
| Chapter 1: Anatomical Language 1                                   |     |
| Chapter 2: Use of the Microscope 30                                |     |
| Chapter 3: Cells 40                                                |     |
| Chapter 4: Histology/Tissues 73                                    |     |
| Chapter 5: Integumentary<br>System 95                              |     |
| Chapter 6: Skeletal Terms, Bone Histology, and Microanatomy 123    |     |
| Chapter 7: Skeletal System –Axial Division 151                     |     |
| Chapter 8: Skeletal System -<br>Appendicular Division 193          |     |
| Chapter 9. The Articulations and Joints 224                        |     |
| Chapter 10: Skeletal Muscle<br>Histology and Microanatomy 245      |     |
| Chapter 11: Muscular system; Gross Anatomy 268                     |     |
| Chapter 12: Nervous System; Microanatomy 305                       |     |
| Chapter 13: Nervous System; Gross Anatomy 326                      |     |
| Chapter 14: Special Senses 375                                     |     |
| Chapter 15: Endocrine System 409                                   |     |
| Chapter 16 Cardiovascular System: The Blood and Heart 440          |     |
| Chapter 17: Cardiovascular system; The Arteries and Veins 474      |     |
| Chapter 18: Lymphatic System 509                                   |     |
| Chapter 19: Respiratory System 531                                 |     |
| Chapter 20: Digestive System 561                                   |     |
| Chapter 21: Urinary System 612                                     |     |
| Chapter 22: Reproductive System: Male Reproductive System 643      |     |
| Chapter 23: Reproductive System: Female Reproductive<br>System 663 |     |
| Attributions 692                                                   |     |

\# **Acknowledgments**

This manual includes original content by George Mateja, Professor of Biology at Community College of Baltimore County. Its creation also contains a variety of remixed and revised content from other resources. These sources are noted in the attributions section for each lab.

A considerable amount of gratitude is owed to those who have offered their time to review this work. They have generously offered their time, expertise, and ideas to assist in the creation and/or editing of this work.

-   Julie Robinson, DC. River Valley Community College
-   Christine Mirbaha, Community College of Baltimore County
-   Michael Losinger, Community College of Baltimore County

I would like to thank the library staff who supported the creation of this work. Their role as advisors to answer questions concerning copyright and attribution questions patiently and earnestly in a timely manner was greatly appreciated.

-   Jenn Ditkoff, Head Librarian, Dundalk
-   Jean Boggs, Reference Librarian
-   Shaune Pyle, OER and Online Learning Librarian
-   Cheryl Kantorski, Administrative Support Assistant III; Library Services

This lab manual was written for a one-semester Anatomy and Physiology course such as the Biology 109 Introduction to Human Biology taught at the Community College of Baltimore County. This workbook is written to complement a onesemester Anatomy and Physiology text. The subject material is based on the course objectives developed by the Community College of Baltimore County's Biology 109 course committee.

The completion of this work was supported by a Community College of Baltimore County Summer Grant during the summer of 2024. A special note of thanks goes to President Sandra Kurtinitis, Ph.D. and the Provost & Vice President of Academics and Student Joaquin Martinez, Ph.D. of the Community College of Baltimore County for their constant support and encouragement for those who create OER and open pedagogy curriculum.

# **Chapter 1: Anatomical language**

## **Your objectives for this lab are to:**

-   Define and demonstrate the anatomical position.
-   Use the correct terminology to describe the planes of the body.
-   Identify the planes of view is several different views of the body.
-   Define and demonstrate the different anatomical positions.
-   Define and demonstrate the directional terms used in anatomy.
-   Define and apply the different anatomical regions.
-   Locate the major cavities and describe several of the organs that lie within each.
-   Identify the abdominopelvic cavities':
-   Four quadrants and their organs
-   Nine regions and their organs
-   Identify the serous membranes within the body and their respective layers.

## **Terms to learn:**

## **Regional Terms**

## *Anterior View*

-   Abdominal

-   Antebrachial

-   Antecubital

-   Axillary

-   Brachial

-   Buccal

-   Carpal

-   Cervical

-   Thoracic

-   Crural

-   Deltoid

-   Digital

-   Femoral

-   Frontal

-   Inguinal

-   Nasal

-   Oral

-   Orbital

-   Palmar

-   Patellar

-   Pectoral

-   Pedal

-   Pelvic

-   Pubic

-   Sternal

-   Tarsal

### **Posterior View**

-   Calcaneal

-   Cephalic

-   Gluteal

-   Lumbar

-   Occipital

-   Plantar

-   Sacral

-   Vertebral

## **Directional Terms**

-   Superior (cranial, cephalic)

-   Inferior (caudal)

-   Anterior (ventral)

-   Posterior (dorsal)

-   Medial

-   Lateral

-   Proximal

-   Distal

-   Intermediate

-   Superficial (external)

-   Deep (internal)

## **Planes**

-   Sagittal

-   Midsagittal

-   Parasagittal

-   Frontal (coronal)

-   Transverse (horizontal)

-   Oblique section

-   Cross-section vs. Longitudinal section

## **Body Cavities, Membranes, and Abdominopelvic Quadrants**

Be able to identify cavities, as well as organs found in each cavity.

## **Cavities**

-   Dorsal cavity
-   Cranial cavity
-   Spinal cavity
-   Ventral cavity
-   Thoracic cavity
-   Pleural cavities
-   Pericardial cavity
-   Mediastinum
-   Abdominopelvic cavity
-   Abdominal cavity
-   Pelvic cavity

## **Abdominopelvic Quadrants – identify organs within each quadrant.**

-   Right upper quadrant

-   Left upper quadrant.

-   Right lower quadrant

-   Left lower quadrant.

## **Organ Systems**

**Know what organs are in each system, as well as the general functions of the system.**

-   Integumentary
-   Skeletal
-   Muscular
-   Nervous
-   Endocrine
-   Cardiovascular
-   Lymphatic
-   Respiratory
-   Digestive
-   Urinary
-   Reproductive

## **Prelab Activities**

## **Prelab Activity 1.1**

Definitions: define the terms that are essential to this chapter.

### Directional terms

## Directional Terms

|                       | Meaning                          |
|-----------------------|----------------------------------|
| Anterior (or ventral) | front                            |
| Posterior (or dorsal) | back                             |
| Superior (or cranial) | upper                            |
| Inferior (or caudal)  | lower                            |
| Lateral               | away from midline                |
| Medial                | toward midline                   |
| Proximal              | closer to point of attachment    |
| Distal                | farther from point of attachment |
| Superficial           | near the surface                 |
| Deep                  | away from the surface            |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Body Cavities

| Cavity      | Location   | Organs                           | Function            |
|---------------|---------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| Cranial     | Skull      | Brain                            | Protects brain      |
| Abdominal   | Abdomen    | Digestive organs                 | Digestion           |
| Pericardial | Chest      | Heart                            | Protects heart      |
| Pleural     | Chest      | Lungs                            | Protects lungs      |
| Ventral     | Front body | Thoracic + abdominopelvic organs | Houses major organs |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Body Planes

| Plane              | Description         | Division           | Example       |
|------------------|-------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Sagittal plane     | Divides left/right  | Unequal halves     | Side view     |
| Midsagittal plane  | Divides equally     | Equal halves       | Midline       |
| Parasagittal plane | Off-center sagittal | Unequal halves     | Slight side   |
| Frontal plane      | Divides front/back  | Anterior/posterior | Face vs back  |
| Transverse plane   | Divides top/bottom  | Superior/inferior  | Cross-section |

Oblique plane **cut at an angle between planes**

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Prelab Activity 1.2 – Identification

| Cavity number | Cavity or structures      |
|---------------|---------------------------|
| 1             | Cranial cavity            |
| 2             | Vertebral (spinal) cavity |
| 3             | Thoracic cavity           |
| 4             | Pleural cavity            |
| 5             | Pericardial cavity        |
| 6             | Abdominal cavity          |
| 7             | Pelvic cavity             |
| a             | Diaphragm                 |
| b             | Mediastinum               |
| c             | Lung                      |
| d             | Heart                     |
| e             | Abdominopelvic cavity     |

\## **Prelab Activity 1.3**

\## **Identification**

Using the lab manual or your textbook, list the 12 organ systems of the body below and the major functions and organs in each: (Note: Male and female reproductive systems are counted as two systems, 11a and 11b, respectively).

**Table 1.2**. Identification of Organs and their Function

\|## Organ Systems

| Organ System | Function | Organs |
|----|----|----|
| 1 Nervous | Controls and coordinates body | Brain |
| 2 Digestive | Breaks down food and absorbs nutrients | Stomach |
| 3 Cardiovascular | Transports blood and nutrients | Heart |
| 4 Respiratory | Gas exchange (O₂/CO₂) | Lungs |
| 5 Muscular | Movement and heat production | Skeletal muscles |
| 6 Skeletal | Support and protection | Bones |
| 7 Endocrine | Hormone regulation | Glands (thyroid) |
| 8 Urinary | Removes waste and regulates fluids | Kidneys |
| 9 Integumentary | Protection and temperature control | Skin |
| 10 Lymphatic | Immune defense | Lymph nodes |
| 11a Male reproductive | Produces sperm | Testes |
| 11b Female reproductive | Produces eggs | Ovaries |

\## **Lab Activities**

\### **Lab Activity 1.1**

\## **Body Organization and Terminology Review**

**Anatomical Terminology**: Using the information in your textbook define the following anatomical terms:

\### **Anatomical position**

![](_page_8_Picture_5.jpeg)

**Figure 1.2**. Anatomical position.

#### **Prone and supine positions**

![](_page_8_Picture_8.jpeg)

**Figure 1.3** Prone and supine diagrams.

## **Applying Directional Terms**; label the diagrams below:

## List of Directional Terms

-   Right – toward the right side\
-   Left – toward the left side\
-   Superior – toward the head/above\
-   Inferior – toward the feet/below\
-   Anterior – front of the body\
-   Posterior – back of the body\
-   Dorsal – back side\
-   Ventral – front side\
-   Cranial – toward the head\
-   Caudal – toward the tail/feet\
-   Medial – toward the midline\
-   Lateral – away from the midline\
-   Proximal – closer to point of attachment\
-   Distal – farther from point of attachment\
-   Superficial – near the surface\
-   Deep – away from the surface

In the image below label the directional terms that are listed above

![](_page_9_Picture_20.jpeg)

**Figure 1.4** Unlabeled directional terms diagram.

Indicate in the boxes provided the deep and superficial positions.

![](_page_10_Picture_1.jpeg)

**Figure 1.5** Sagittal section of the head. The subject is an 18-year-old male who had blunt trauma to the head after a 25 m long jump during [motocross.](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Motocross)

## **Lab Activity 1.3**

## **Labeling Anatomical and Regional Terminology**

-   Obtain labeling tape and one of the models or skeletons ( e.g., a torso model, mini muscle person torso, skeleton)
-   Write the directional terms from activity 1.2 on the model.
-   Once you are done, have another group check your accuracy. Make corrections if needed.
-   Then, have your instructor or Lab Instructor check your accuracy.
-   Take pictures of your work to use as a study guide.

## **Applying Anatomical and Regional Terminology**

Use appropriate anatomical and regional terminology to fill in the blanks in the statements below.

## Directional Terms – Fill in the Blank

| Index | Fill in the blank |
|------------------------------------|------------------------------------|
| 1 | The elbow is located **proximal** to the wrist. |
| 2 | The umbilicus is located **inferior** to the sternum. |
| 3 | The nose is located **medial** to the ears. |
| 4 | The mouth is located **superior** to the chin. |
| 5 | A papercut would be considered **superficial**; a puncture wound would be **deep**. |
| 6 | A wound on the elbow would be **distal** to the shoulder. |

\## **Lab Activity 1.5**

## Applying Anatomical and Regional Terminology

| Starting Position | Instructions Given to Move to Anatomical Position |
|---|---|
| **1: From lying face-up on the ground with their head, back, hands, and feet on the floor with both knees bent (Figure 1.7)** | • Extend both knees inferiorly until the lower limbs are straight.<br>• Position the upper limbs at the sides of the trunk.<br>• Rotate the palmar regions anteriorly.<br>• Position the head and torso facing anteriorly. |
| **2: From a seated position on the floor with their legs straight and arms folded across their chest (Figure 1.8)** | • Extend the torso superiorly into a standing position.<br>• Position the upper limbs at the sides of the trunk.<br>• Rotate the palmar regions anteriorly.<br>• Position the lower limbs together and facing anteriorly.<br>• Position the head facing anteriorly. |

## Sectioning/Body Planes

### Body Planes

- Sagittal plane – divides the body into left and right portions

- Midsagittal plane – divides the body into equal left and right halves

- Parasagittal plane – divides the body into unequal left and right portions

- Transverse plane – divides the body into superior and inferior portions

- Coronal plane – divides the body into anterior and posterior portions

- Oblique plane – diagonal cut through the body or limb (draw diagonally across the right brachial region)
![](_page_13_Figure_10.jpeg)

## Sectioning/Body Planes

### Figure 1.10 Human Head Slice

Plane: **Transverse plane**

### Figure 1.11 An Anatomical Illustration from Sobotta's Human Anatomy 1908

Plane: **Midsagittal plane**

## Regional Terminology

- Obtain tape and any of the following models:
  - A torso model
  - Mini muscle person torso
  - Skeleton

- Write the following terms and attach them to your models.

## **Regions of the body,**

## Axial Region

- **Cephalic (head)**
  - Cranial
  - Facial
  - Frontal (forehead)
  - Orbital (eye)
  - Nasal (nose)
  - Buccal (cheek)
  - Oral (mouth)
  - Mental (chin)
  - Cervical/Nuchal (neck)

## Appendicular Region

### Upper Extremity

- Axillary (armpit)
- Brachial (arm)
- Cubital (elbow)
- Antebrachial (forearm)
- Carpal (wrist)
- Manual (hand)
- Digital (finger)

## Trunk

- Thoracic (chest)
- Sternal
- Clavicular
- Acromial (shoulder)
- Abdominal (belly)
- Inguinal (groin)
- Pubic (genital)
- Coxal (hip)
- Vertebral (vertebral column)
- Lumbar (lower back)
- Sacral
- Gluteal (buttocks)

## Lower Extremity

- Femoral (thigh)
- Popliteal (back of knee)
- Patellar (kneecap)
- Crural (leg)
- Calcaneal (heel)
- Tarsal (ankle)
- Pedal (foot)

## Body Cavities and Associated Serous Membranes

### Dorsal Cavity

- Cranial cavity
- Vertebral cavity

### Ventral Cavity

- Thoracic cavity
  - Mediastinum
  - Pleural cavities
  - Pericardial cavity
  - Diaphragm

- Abdominopelvic cavity
  - Abdominal cavity
  - Pelvic cavity

## **Body Cavities and Associated Serous Membranes Continued**

## **Viscera:**

Ventral body cavities contain **membranes** = soft, thin, pliable layer of tissue:

-   **Visceral Membranes** cover the organs
-   **Parietal Membranes** line the body cavity
    -   o There is a space between a visceral and parietal membrane, the **serous cavity**, into which **serous fluid** is secreted for **lubrication**.
-   Cavities referenced to the membranes.
    -   o Pleura
    -   o Pericardial
    -   o Peritoneum

![](_page_16_Picture_10.jpeg)

**Figure 1.13** Labeled body cavities.

## **Abdominopelvic Cavity Regions and Quadrants**

-   [**Abdominal**](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=29624323Abdominal) **quadrants**
    -   o Right upper quadrant
    -   o Right lower quadrant
    -   o Left upper quadrant
    -   o Left Lower Quadrant
-   **Abdominal regions**
    -   o Epigastric
    -   o Left and right hypochondriac
    -   o Umbilical
    -   o Left and right lumbar
    -   o Hypogastric
    -   o Left and right iliac

![](_page_17_Figure_14.jpeg)

**Figure 1.14** Abdominal quadrants and regions with labels for identification.

## **Learning the Quadrants and Planes**

-   Obtain a large torso model.
-   Using the information in activity 1.7 and the torso:
    -   o Properly identify the quadrant and the region
    -   o Fill in the tables below. If there is more than one organ in a cavity, list at least two organs.

#### **Table 1.3. [Abdominal](https://commons.wikimedia.org/w/index.php?curid=29624323Abdominal) Quadrants**

## Abdominal Quadrants

| Quadrants   | Organs                    |
|-------------|---------------------------|
| Right upper | Liver, gallbladder        |
| Right lower | Appendix, small intestine |
| Left upper  | Stomach, spleen           |
| Left lower  | Colon, small intestine    |

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Table 1.4. Abdominal Regions

| Regions             | Organs           |
|---------------------|------------------|
| left hypochondriac  | Spleen           |
| Epigastric          | Stomach          |
| right hypochondriac | Liver            |
| left lumbar         | Descending colon |
| umbilical           | Small intestine  |
| right lumbar        | Ascending colon  |
| left iliac          | Sigmoid colon    |
| hypogastric         | Bladder          |
| right iliac         | Appendix         |

![](_page_19_Figure_0.jpeg)

Figure 1.15 Organ Systems I.

![](_page_20_Figure_0.jpeg)

Figure 1.16 Organ Systems II.

## **Lab Activity 1.9: Organ Systems**

Using your textbook, list the 12 organ systems of the body below and the organs in each: (Note: Male and female reproductive systems are counted as two systems here (i.e., 11a and 11b).

**Table 1.5 Organ systems and their Organs**

## Organ Systems (Organs)

| Organ System            | Organs           |
|-------------------------|------------------|
| 1 Cardiovascular        | Heart            |
| 2 Respiratory           | Lungs            |
| 3 Digestive             | Stomach          |
| 4 Nervous               | Brain            |
| 5 Muscular              | Skeletal muscles |
| 6 Skeletal              | Bones            |
| 7 Endocrine             | Thyroid gland    |
| 8 Urinary               | Kidneys          |
| 9 Integumentary         | Skin             |
| 10 Lymphatic            | Lymph nodes      |
| 11a Male reproductive   | Testes           |
| 11b Female reproductive | Ovaries          |

# **Post Lab Activity: Review material**

## Post Lab Activity 1.1 – Answers

### Systems

-   The system forms the external body covering → Integumentary system\
-   The system protects deeper tissues from injury → Integumentary system\
-   The system synthesizes vitamin D → Integumentary system\
-   Organs secrete hormones → Endocrine system\
-   Keeps blood supplied with oxygen and removes CO₂ → Respiratory system\
-   Produces sperm/eggs and sex hormones → Reproductive system

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Homeostasis

-   Amplified feedback → Positive feedback\
-   Returned to normal → Negative feedback

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Position Terms Review

-   Knees to ankles → Superior\
-   Thumbs to pinky → Lateral\
-   Spine to breastbone → Posterior\
-   Chest to shoulder blades → Anterior\
-   Pinky to thumbs → Medial\
-   Navel to lower back → Anterior\
-   Eyes to nose → Lateral\
-   Breasts to lungs → Superficial\
-   Nose to neck → Superior\
-   Elbows to wrists → Proximal\
-   Mouth to forehead → Inferior\
-   Calf to shin → Posterior\
-   Genitals to hips → Medial\
-   Ankles to shins → Distal\
-   Lips to ears → Medial\
-   Brain to skull → Deep\
-   Lower back to navel → Posterior\
-   Thighs to feet → Proximal\
-   Ribcage to lungs → Superficial\
-   Skin to muscles → Superficial

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Regional Terms

-   Nasal → nose\
-   Palmar → palm\
-   Oral → mouth\
-   Digital → fingers/toes\
-   Cervical → neck\
-   Pubic → genital region\
-   Acromial → shoulder\
-   Patellar → knee\
-   Axillary → armpit\
-   Crural → leg\
-   Abdominal → abdomen\
-   Pedal → foot\
-   Brachial → arm\
-   Tarsal → ankle\
-   Antecubital → front of elbow\
-   Frontal → forehead\
-   Antebrachial → forearm\
-   Orbital → eye\
-   Pelvic → pelvis\
-   Buccal → cheek\
-   Carpal → wrist

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Planes

-   Frontal (coronal) plane → divides front/back\
-   Transverse plane → divides top/bottom

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Cavities

-   Cranial → dorsal cavity\
-   Spinal → dorsal cavity\
-   Thoracic → ventral cavity\
-   Abdominopelvic → ventral cavity\
-   Brain → cranial cavity\
-   Spinal cord → vertebral cavity

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Post Lab Activity 1.2 – Multiple Choice

1.  

    d)  Frontal plane\

2.  

    b)  Thoracic and abdominal cavities\

3.  

    c)  Midsagittal plane\

4.  

    b)  Mediastinum\

5.  

    c)  Umbilical region\

6.  

    d)  Endocrine system\

7.  

    d)  Skeletal system\

8.  

    d)  None of the above\

9.  

    d)  Visceral\

10. 

    c)  Lateral\
        \## **Post Lab Activity 1.3: Crossword Puzzles**

![](_page_25_Picture_1.jpeg)

**Figure 1.17** Systems of the body.

## Crossword – Organ Systems (Across)

-   Endocrine\
-   Cardiovascular\
-   Integumentary\
-   Muscular\
-   Reproductive\
-   Nervous\
-   Urinary\
-   Digestive\
-   Skeletal\
-   Respiratory\
    ![](_page_26_Picture_0.jpeg)

## Crossword – Body Cavities (Across)

-   4 Thoracic\

-   5 Pleural\

-   1 Mediastinum\

-   2 Pelvic\

-   3 Abdominal\
    \## **Additional Learning Resources: (Robinson, 2019)**

-   **Watch** these three (3) videos to learn more about these critical, introductory concepts of A&P listed above.

    -   o [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D4vayAF4atI&feature=yout](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D4vayAF4atI&feature=youtu.be) [u.be](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D4vayAF4atI&feature=youtu.be)
    -   o [https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4UJ4sylQsEM&feature=yo](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4UJ4sylQsEM&feature=youtu.be) [utu.be](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4UJ4sylQsEM&feature=youtu.be)
    -   o Review of anatomical position, directional terms, and planes/sections. <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f6rZw7QkGLw> -

-   **Practice** anatomical terminology and labeling of regions, cavities, and membranes here:

    -   o <https://webanatomy.umn.edu/ch1-topics> (regions and cavities)

### **Other resources**

-   **OpenStax:**
    -   o <https://openstax.org/details/anatomy-and-physiology> OpenStax Anatomy & Physiology e-book. Not to be used as replacement for the required textbook but as an additional source of information, including chapter questions at the end of chapters. Unless otherwise noted, all content on [Open](http://faq.openoregon.org/openoregon.org) Oregon [Educational](http://faq.openoregon.org/openoregon.org) Resources is licensed under a [Creative](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/) Commons Attribution 4.0 [International](http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/4.0/) License.
-   **KenHub:**
    -   o [https://www.kenhub.com](https://www.kenhub.com/) Online anatomy tutorials, videos, and quizzes. Free registration. Many may be more detailed than might be needed at this level but professionally created and accurate.
-   **Miscellaneous:**
    -   o [http://www.sumanasinc.com/webcontent/animations/biology.ht](http://www.sumanasinc.com/webcontent/animations/biology.html) [ml](http://www.sumanasinc.com/webcontent/animations/biology.html) – many links to video animations that have simple animations and explanations for some processes (i.e., Synaptic transmission, Action potential conduction (explaining changes that occur in voltage-controlled Na gates, etc.)

### **Answer Keys:**

### **Crosswords:**

**Systems of the body**

-   **Across: 2** Endocrine, **6** Cardiovascular, **8** Integumentary, **10** Muscular.
-   **Down: 1** Reproductive, **3** Nervous, **4** Urinary, **5** Digestive, **7** Skeletal, **9** Respiratory.

### **Body cavities:**

-   **Across: 4** Thoracic, **5** Pleural.
-   **Down: 1** Mediastinum, **2** Pelvic, **3** Abdominal.

## Chapter 1 – Anatomical Language Glossary (Simplified)

-   Abdominal – relating to the abdomen\

-   Abdominal cavity – contains digestive organs\

-   Abdominopelvic cavity – contains abdominal + pelvic organs\

-   Anabolism – builds complex molecules\

-   Anatomical position – standard body position for reference\

-   Anatomy – study of body structure

-   Anterior (ventral) – front\

-   Posterior (dorsal) – back\

-   Superior (cranial) – above\

-   Inferior (caudal) – below

-   Medial – toward midline\

-   Lateral – away from midline\

-   Proximal – closer to attachment\

-   Distal – farther from attachment\

-   Superficial – near surface\

-   Deep – away from surface

-   Cranial cavity – contains brain\

-   Spinal cavity – contains spinal cord\

-   Thoracic cavity – contains heart + lungs\

-   Abdominopelvic cavity – contains digestive + pelvic organs

-   Frontal plane – divides front/back\

-   Sagittal plane – divides left/right\

-   Transverse plane – divides top/bottom

-   Homeostasis – stable internal environment\

-   Negative feedback – returns to normal\

-   Positive feedback – amplifies change

-   Cell – smallest living unit\

-   Tissue – group of similar cells\

-   Organ – group of tissues\

-   Organ system – group of organs\

-   Organism – living being

-   Nervous system – control + communication\

-   Endocrine system – hormones\

-   Cardiovascular system – transport\

-   Respiratory system – gas exchange\

-   Digestive system – breaks down food\

-   Muscular system – movement\

-   Skeletal system – support\

-   Integumentary system – protection\

-   Urinary system – waste removal\

-   Reproductive system – reproduction\

-   Lymphatic system – immunity

-   Serous membrane – reduces friction\

-   Pleura – lungs\

-   Pericardium – heart\

-   Peritoneum – abdominal organs

-   Prone – face down\

-   Supine – face up
